Part A — Problem Specification

Criterion 1 · Observation 1 · Design artefact (no fixed word count)
Your task here is precision, not length. A single well-defined ADT operation signature is worth more than two paragraphs of vague description. Use the Graph Explorer above to help you understand what you are modelling.

A1. Algorithmic problem statement

Formulate the algorithmic problem precisely. A strong problem statement has three parts: inputs, outputs, and constraints — stated formally, not in narrative form. Guiding questions:
  • What data does the algorithm receive? (Not "the facility" — be specific: what type, what structure?)
  • What does a correct output look like? Is it a path? A sequence of decisions? A cost?
  • Which of the mission constraints from Memo 01 must appear in the problem specification?
  • Can you state a constraint as a mathematical inequality or a set membership condition?
Tip: Try writing your problem statement in the form: "Given [inputs], find [output] such that [constraints are satisfied]."

Each Vertex in V is a sector and each edge in E is a corridor between two sectors.\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThis can be represented as:\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eG = (V,E)\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eV = {v | v is a sector in the Emberlight Subterranean Research Complex}\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eE = {(u,v) | u,v∈V ∧ u is adjacent to v ∧ direct movement can be taken from u into v, without going through any other sectors}\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003ch4 id=\"set-1-supply-units-su\"\u003eSet 1 (Supply Units (SU):\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThis is a set containing all supply units in the Sector Grid. \nSU = {x | x is a supply unit in the Sector Grid}.\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003ch4 id=\"set-2-autonomous-systems-as\"\u003eSet 2 (Autonomous Systems (AS)):\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThis is a set containing all Autonomous Systems (AS) being deployed in the Sector Grid.\nAS = {x | x is an Autonomous System being deployed in the Sector Grid}\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003ch4 id=\"note-on-maps\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eNOTE on Maps:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThe following four ADTs are maps of maps, where the key gives you a map of properties of that object. For example if the edge has no cost of traversal, it will not include a key associated with it. \u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eIn the current problem scope, a possible implementation of this is: \nEP = {(u,v):{ \"cardinal_angle\": real number (cardinal angle from u to v)}\n        | u,v ∈ V ∧ (u,v) ∈ E}\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003ch4 id=\"map-2-sector-properties-vp\"\u003eMap 2 (Sector Properties (VP)):\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThis map, VP (Vertex Properties) takes vertices (or sectors) as keys and returns a map potentially containing the properties:\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003ea string indicating what supply unit lies within the sector, or null if no supply unit is contained, (if not specified it can be assumed no supply unit is in the sector).\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003ea Boolean value indicating whether it is an entry (assume False if not specified),\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003ea Boolean value indicating whether it is an exit (assume False if not specified).\nBeyond current scope:\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003ea value indicating the stability of the sector.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003esize\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eshape\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eIn the current problem scope, a possible implementation of this is: \nVP = {v : { \n        \"supply_unit\": nullable string (name of supply unit within the sector, null if it doesn't contain a supply unit),\n        \"is_entry\": Boolean (true if the sector is an entry, false otherwise) ,\n        \"is_exit\": Boolean (true if the sector is an extraction point, false otherwise )}\n        | v ∈ V}\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003ch4 id=\"map-3-supply-unit-properties-sup\"\u003eMap 3 (Supply Unit Properties (SUP)):\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eThis map, SUP (Supply Unit Properties), takes a supply unit as a key, and returns a map of the properties of the supply unit. data.text/markdown:
A2. Salient features

Identify which features of the real-world scenario must be captured in the model, and which can be safely abstracted away. Guiding questions:
  • A "salient feature" is one that, if omitted, would make your solution incorrect or incomplete. What makes a feature salient here?
  • The corridors are physical tunnels, but in your model they become graph edges. What properties of the tunnels are salient? What can be ignored?
  • CRUDY-1 has a physical position in the facility. How is "current position" represented in your model?
  • Time is not modelled explicitly in Memo 01. Is that a safe abstraction? Why or why not?
  • The supply units have a physical mass, but in Memo 01 they are treated as uniform. Is this a salient simplification? What might break if mass were introduced later? (Hint: look ahead to Memo 03.)
Format: For each salient feature, state (a) what it is, (b) how it is modelled, and (c) what is deliberately abstracted away and why.

A3. Data model — ADT selection and signatures

Describe the Abstract Data Types you will use to model the Emberlight facility. For each ADT, state: (1) what real-world entity it models, (2) why it is the most appropriate ADT for that entity, and (3) the signatures of its key operations. Signature format used in Algorithmics: 
operation_name(param: Type, ...) → ReturnType
Example — for a Graph ADT: 
add_vertex(v: Vertex) → None
add_edge(u: Vertex, v: Vertex) → None
neighbours(v: Vertex) → Set[Vertex]
has_edge(u: Vertex, v: Vertex) → Boolean
vertices() → Set[Vertex]
Guiding questions:
  • Which ADT models the facility layout? Which models the drone's route? Are these the same ADT or different ones?
  • A Queue and a Stack are both linear ADTs. What is the key behavioural difference, and does that difference matter for your algorithm?
  • Do you need an ADT to track which supply units have been collected? What operations would it need?
  • Is there an ADT that tracks the drone's "path so far"? What operations does it need that a plain list might not provide cleanly?
  • For each operation you define: what is the precondition (when is it valid to call it)? What is the postcondition (what is guaranteed after it runs)?

How does the absence of cycles affect the uniqueness of\n  paths between nodes?\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eLook up the term \u003cstrong\u003egraph isomorphism\u003c/strong\u003e. data.text/markdown:
B1. Consideration of approaches

Consider at least two algorithm design approaches. For each, explain the design pattern, assess its suitability for this problem, and identify its limitations. Then clearly justify the approach you selected. Approaches to consider (you are not limited to these):
  • Uninformed search: BFS, DFS
  • Informed search: Greedy best-first, A*
  • Greedy strategies: nearest-unvisited, nearest-supply-first
Guiding questions for each approach:
  • What is the strategy of this algorithm — what decision rule does it use at each step?
  • Does it guarantee finding a path? Does it guarantee finding the shortest path?
  • What data structure drives it (queue, stack, priority queue)?
  • How well does it handle the dual objective: collect supplies and reach an exit?
  • Does using BFS or DFS on your specific facility (from the tracer above) produce a sensible path? Describe what you observed.
For your selected approach:
  • Why is it more appropriate than the alternatives for this specific problem?
  • What are its known weaknesses, and are those weaknesses acceptable here?

B2. Algorithm description

Describe your chosen algorithm in plain English before expressing it as pseudocode. This narrative should make your design intent clear to a reader who hasn't seen your code. Guiding questions:
  • How does CRUDY-1 decide which sector to move to next?
  • In what order does it visit the supply units? Is there a strategy to the order?
  • How does the algorithm know when to head for the exit rather than collecting more supplies?
  • How does the algorithm use the ADT operations you defined in A3? Name at least two ADT operations and describe the role they play.
  • Is there a sub-problem within your algorithm that could be separated into its own function? What would that function do?

data.text/html: <marimo-callout-output data-html='"\u003cspan class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"\u003e\u003ch3 id=\"research-inquiry-part-b\"\u003e🔬 Research &amp; Inquiry — Part B\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eSearch algorithms — going deeper:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eDijkstra's algorithm\u003c/strong\u003e finds the shortest path in a \u003cem\u003eweighted\u003c/em\u003e graph. BFS only works\n  on \u003cem\u003eunweighted\u003c/em\u003e graphs (or graphs where all edges have equal weight). Look up Dijkstra's\n  algorithm — how does using a priority queue change the order of exploration?\n  \u003cem\u003e(This will become relevant in Memo 02 when edge weights are introduced.)\u003c/em\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eA* search\u003c/strong\u003e is Dijkstra's algorithm with a \u003cem\u003eheuristic\u003c/em\u003e — an estimate of the remaining\n  cost to the goal. A good heuristic for a grid graph is the Manhattan distance.\n  Could you use Manhattan distance as a heuristic for the Emberlight problem? What are\n  the conditions for a heuristic to be \u003cem\u003eadmissible\u003c/em\u003e?\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eThe Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):\u003c/strong\u003e collecting all five supply units before\n  reaching an exit is related to TSP — finding the shortest route that visits a set\n  of locations. TSP is NP-hard. Does that mean your problem is NP-hard? Why or why not?\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eCompleteness and optimality:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eAn algorithm is \u003cstrong\u003ecomplete\u003c/strong\u003e if it always finds a solution when one exists.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eAn algorithm is \u003cstrong\u003eoptimal\u003c/strong\u003e if it always finds the \u003cem\u003ebest\u003c/em\u003e solution.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eIs your chosen algorithm complete? Is it optimal? These are separate questions —\n  an algorithm can be complete but not optimal (e.g. DFS finds \u003cem\u003ea\u003c/em\u003e path, not the \u003cem\u003eshortest\u003c/em\u003e).\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eReal-world connections:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eResearch \"robot path planning\" and \"occupancy grid navigation\". How do real\n  autonomous systems handle environments that are not fully known in advance?\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eLook up \"Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)\". This technique is used in game-playing AI\n  (e.g. AlphaGo). Could it be applied to the Emberlight problem? What would the\n  \"game tree\" look like?\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\u003c/span\u003e"' data-kind='"neutral"'></marimo-callout-output>

A strong justification does not just assert that your solution is good. It argues — using evidence from your algorithm's design and behaviour — that it meets the mission requirements.

D1. Justification

Justify your chosen solution by addressing all three dimensions below. You must engage with the specific Emberlight scenario — generic statements about your algorithm that could apply to any problem will not be sufficient. 1. Suitability Why is this algorithm appropriate for this problem?
  • What properties of the Emberlight facility make your algorithm a natural fit?
  • Consider: does the facility structure (tree, grid, dead-end placements) favour your approach? Use evidence from the Graph Explorer.
  • Are there properties of the problem that your algorithm handles better than the alternatives you considered in B1?
2. Coherence How does your algorithm integrate with your data model?
  • Name two ADT operations from Part A and explain exactly how they are used in your algorithm (not just "I use a graph" — be specific about which operation, when it is called, and what it returns).
  • Are there any tensions between your ADT design and your algorithm? For example, does your algorithm require an operation that isn't in your ADT signature?
3. Fitness for purpose Does your solution meet the mission directive?
  • Go through each operational constraint from Memo 01 and state explicitly whether your solution satisfies it, and how.
  • Are there any constraints your solution fails to satisfy? If so, acknowledge this honestly and explain whether it is an acceptable trade-off.
